question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted blockTimestamp equal to the exact nanosecond of a deletion event so that the entity_history branch (which lacks a 'deleted is not true' filter) returns a deleted entity's state, causing smart contract calls to observe a historically-deleted account as active and reorganizing the apparent transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the missing 'deleted is not true' guard on the entity_history branch of the UNION query to retrieve a deleted entity's historical record at a blockTimestamp after deletion, making the mirror node report the entity as existing at that point in time and distorting the historical view of account lifecycle?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressOrAliasAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user pass a blockTimestamp that falls within a deleted entity's historical timestamp_range to trigger the entity_history branch (which has no 'deleted is not true' check) and receive a deleted entity's state, causing the historical query to misrepresent the entity as active at that block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user craft a blockTimestamp of Long.MAX_VALUE to force the historical query to return the most recent entity_history record regardless of actual block context, effectively bypassing the intended historical scoping and presenting a future-state entity as the historical truth?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressOrAliasAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user pass a blockTimestamp of 0 to the historical query so that the entity_cte CTE (which filters 'created_timestamp <= ?2') returns no ID, causing the UNION to silently return empty and making a legitimately existing entity appear non-existent at that historical point?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByIdAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user supply a blockTimestamp exactly equal to lower(timestamp_range) of a historical record to exploit the '<=' boundary condition and retrieve an entity state that was valid only for a single nanosecond, reorganizing the apparent history of that entity's attributes at that block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the entity_cte selecting only from the 'entity' table (not entity_history) to cause the CTE to return no ID when the entity has been fully moved to history, making the UNION return empty even though the entity existed at the queried blockTimestamp, thus erasing it from historical view?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressOrAliasAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user craft a byte array that matches the alias of entity A but also matches the evm_address of entity B, exploiting the OR condition 'evm_address = ?1 or alias = ?1' in the entity_cte to resolve to the wrong entity ID and retrieve a different entity's historical state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddressAndDeletedIsFalse()] Can an unprivileged user craft two different byte arrays that produce the same @spelHelper.hashCode() value, causing a cache collision in CACHE_NAME_EVM_ADDRESS so that a lookup for one EVM address returns the cached entity of a different address, reorganizing which entity is seen as active?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findByEvmAddressOrAliasAndDeletedIsFalse()] Can an unprivileged user craft two byte arrays with the same @spelHelper.hashCode() to poison the CACHE_NAME_ALIAS cache, causing subsequent callers to receive a cached entity that does not correspond to their queried alias, effectively reorganizing which entity is resolved for a given alias?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findByIdAndDeletedIsFalse()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the dual-cache condition (#entityId < 1000 && !isBalanceCallSafe()) to cause a system account entity to be stored in CACHE_MANAGER_SYSTEM_ACCOUNT but not CACHE_MANAGER_ENTITY (or vice versa), creating an inconsistency where different callers see different entity states for the same system account ID?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the fact that the entity_cte uses 'order by created_timestamp desc limit 1' to always pick the most recently created entity with a given evm_address, even if an older entity with the same address existed at the queried blockTimestamp, causing the wrong entity's history to be returned?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByIdAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user query with a blockTimestamp just before an entity's creation (lower(timestamp_range) - 1) to confirm the entity did not exist at that point, then use this to enumerate the exact creation timestamp of any entity by binary-searching blockTimestamp values, leaking historical creation metadata?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByIdAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the 'deleted is not true' check (which allows NULL deleted values) to retrieve entities whose deleted column is NULL, causing entities in an ambiguous deletion state to appear as active in historical queries and distorting the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user exploit the 'deleted is not true' check on the entity branch (which passes NULL deleted) combined with the missing deleted check on entity_history to cause a scenario where a NULL-deleted entity in entity_history is returned as active at a historical timestamp?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: findActiveByEvmAddressOrAliasAndTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged user pass an all-zero byte array as the alias parameter to match entities whose evm_address or alias is all zeros, potentially resolving to a system or treasury account and retrieving its historical state at an arbitrary blockTimestamp?
